programs.name: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Chapter 33)
programs.eligibility: Veterans who served at least 90 days on active duty on or after September 11, 2001. Benefit percentage scales with time served.
programs.coverage: Tuition and fees paid directly to school (up to in-state public rates), monthly housing allowance (E-5 w/dependents BAH rate), book stipend up to $1,000/year. Up to 36 months of benefits.
programs.transferable: Yes — can transfer to spouse or dependent children with 6+ years of service and 4-year commitment.
programs.notes: Most valuable education benefit. Use it last — exhaust TA and in-service programs first. Benefits don't expire for those discharged after Jan 1, 2013.

programs.name: Montgomery GI Bill (Chapter 30)
programs.eligibility: Service members who opted in and paid $1,200 buy-in during first year of service.
programs.coverage: Monthly stipend paid to the student (not the school). Rate depends on enrollment status. Up to 36 months.
programs.transferable: No — cannot be transferred to dependents.
programs.notes: Generally less valuable than Post-9/11 GI Bill. Can switch from Montgomery to Post-9/11, but not back. If switching, understand the refund rules — they're complicated.

programs.name: Chapter 35 — Dependents' Educational Assistance (DEA)
programs.eligibility: Spouse or child of a veteran who is permanently and totally disabled (100% P&T), died of a service-connected disability, or was captured/detained by a foreign government.
programs.coverage: Monthly stipend (~$1,574/mo for full-time as of 2026). Paid directly to the student. Up to 36 months of benefits.
programs.transferable: No — this is a separate entitlement for each eligible dependent. Does NOT consume the veteran's GI Bill.
programs.notes: This is independent of the GI Bill. Your kids can use Ch. 35 AND you keep your GI Bill. Key benefit for 100% P&T families. Stacks with state programs like Conroy (MD), Hazlewood (TX), etc.

programs.name: Fry Scholarship (Chapter 33)
programs.eligibility: Child or surviving spouse of a service member who died in the line of duty on or after September 10, 2001.
programs.coverage: Same as Post-9/11 GI Bill — tuition/fees, housing allowance, book stipend. Up to 36 months.
programs.notes: Cannot be used simultaneously with Ch. 35 DEA — must choose one. Typically more generous than DEA.

programs.name: Veteran Readiness and Employment (VR&E / Chapter 31)
programs.eligibility: Veterans with a service-connected disability rating of at least 10% and an employment barrier.
programs.coverage: Tuition, fees, books, supplies, and a monthly subsistence allowance. Can also cover tools, equipment, and job placement services.
programs.notes: Broader than just education — includes career counseling, resume help, job placement. Can be used in combination with GI Bill in some cases. Worth exploring if you have any service-connected disability.

programs.name: Tuition Assistance (TA)
programs.eligibility: Active duty service members. Branch-specific requirements (time in service, command approval, etc.).
programs.coverage: $250 per semester credit hour, capped at $4,500 per fiscal year. Paid directly to the school.
programs.notes: Use this FIRST for your bachelor's degree. Does not reduce GI Bill entitlement. Some schools match TA at $250/credit, making tuition effectively free.

programs.name: Yellow Ribbon Program
programs.agency: U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs + Participating Schools
programs.eligibility: Veterans eligible for 100% Post-9/11 GI Bill benefits attending a participating school where tuition exceeds the GI Bill cap.
programs.coverage: School agrees to cover a portion of excess tuition, VA matches it. Can eliminate out-of-pocket costs at expensive private schools.
programs.transferable: Applies to transferred benefits as well, if the dependent qualifies for 100% benefit level.
programs.notes: Not all schools participate, and participating schools set their own caps. Check the VA's Yellow Ribbon school finder before enrolling.

programs.name: Edith Nourse Rogers STEM Scholarship
programs.eligibility: Post-9/11 GI Bill or Fry Scholarship recipients enrolled in a STEM degree program who have exhausted or are about to exhaust their benefits.
programs.coverage: Up to 9 additional months of Post-9/11 GI Bill benefits (or up to $30,000).
programs.notes: Specifically for students in science, technology, engineering, math, or certain healthcare programs. Must have 6 months or fewer of remaining entitlement.

programs.name: TRICARE (Active Duty / Retiree)
programs.eligibility: Active duty (TRICARE Prime, no cost), retirees (TRICARE Prime/Select, enrollment fees apply), dependents.
programs.coverage: Medical, dental, pharmacy coverage. Multiple plan options with varying costs and provider networks.
programs.transferable: Covers eligible dependents enrolled in DEERS.
programs.notes: TRICARE For Life kicks in at 65 (with Medicare Part B). TAMP provides 180 days of transitional coverage after qualifying separations.

programs.name: VA Healthcare
programs.eligibility: Veterans enrolled in VA healthcare. Priority based on service-connected disability rating, income, and other factors.
programs.coverage: Medical, mental health, pharmacy, some dental. Copays vary by priority group. 100% P&T = no copays.
programs.transferable: No — veteran only. CHAMPVA available for dependents of 100% P&T veterans.
programs.notes: Enroll even if you have other insurance. VA healthcare can serve as primary or supplemental coverage.

programs.name: CHAMPVA
programs.eligibility: Spouse or child of a veteran who is 100% permanently and totally disabled, or who died from a service-connected condition.
programs.coverage: Shares cost of covered healthcare services. Covers most medically necessary services.
programs.notes: If eligible for TRICARE, you must use TRICARE (CHAMPVA is secondary). Good safety net for dependents of P&T veterans who don't have employer insurance.

programs.name: VA Disability Compensation
programs.eligibility: Veterans with service-connected disabilities. Rating from 0% to 100%.
programs.coverage: Monthly tax-free payment based on disability rating and number of dependents. 100% with dependents = ~$4,000+/mo (2026 rates).
programs.notes: File claims before separation using BDD (180-90 days out). Use a VSO — they're free and know the system. 100% P&T unlocks additional benefits (Ch. 35, property tax exemptions, CHAMPVA, commissary access).

programs.name: Thrift Savings Plan (TSP)
programs.eligibility: All uniformed service members. BRS members receive matching contributions.
programs.coverage: Tax-advantaged retirement savings. Traditional and Roth options. Six fund choices (G, F, C, S, I, L).
programs.transferable: Can roll over to IRA or 401(k) after separation.
programs.notes: Lowest expense ratios in the industry. Start contributing early — compound interest is your biggest weapon. BRS members: contribute at least 5% to get full match.

programs.name: Military Pension (20-Year Retirement)
programs.eligibility: Service members who complete 20+ years of active duty service.
programs.coverage: Legacy/High-3: 2.5% × years of service × average of highest 36 months of base pay. BRS: 2.0% × years × high-3, plus TSP matching.
programs.transferable: Survivor Benefit Plan (SBP) provides up to 55% of pension to surviving spouse.
programs.notes: Pension is taxable income (unlike VA disability). CRDP allows 20-year retirees with 50%+ VA disability to receive both pension and disability without offset.

programs.name: SkillBridge
programs.eligibility: Service members within 180 days of separation. Command approval required.
programs.coverage: Work for a civilian employer full-time while still receiving military pay and benefits. Essentially a paid internship/apprenticeship.
programs.notes: Best kept secret in the military. Companies get a free trial employee, you get experience and often a job offer. Start researching 12+ months before your separation date.

programs.name: Transition Assistance Program (TAP)
programs.agency: Department of Defense / Department of Labor
programs.eligibility: All separating and retiring service members. Mandatory attendance.
programs.coverage: Workshops on resume writing, job searching, VA benefits, financial planning, entrepreneurship.
programs.notes: Start early — don't wait until your last month. The earlier you engage with TAP resources, the smoother your transition will be.

programs.name: USMAP (United Services Military Apprenticeship Program)
programs.agency: Department of Defense / Department of Labor
programs.eligibility: Active duty service members in qualifying ratings/MOSes.
programs.coverage: Converts on-the-job military training into nationally recognized Department of Labor apprenticeship certificates.
programs.notes: Free credential that validates your military experience for civilian employers. If your job qualifies, sign up early — it tracks hours over time.
